Catalog Description

Cloud Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Operating system and distributed systems fields

that form the basis of cloud computing such as virtualization, key-value storage

solutions, group membership, failure detection, peer to peer systems, datacenter

networking, resource management and scalability; popular frameworks such as

MapReduce and HDFS and case studies on failure determination. Prerequisite:

Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

Course Learning Outcomes

At the conclusion of this course, students should be able to:

● Discuss the concepts of cloud computing

● Utilize key underlying technologies such as virtualization

● Design and build simple architectures in cloud

● Monitor and manage lifecycle of virtual entities in cloud using tooling from

a major cloud provider

● Produce highly available, elastic and resilient systems using cloud

architectural patterns

● Analyze problems and solutions to become an entry level cloud

practitioner

Special Course Designation

None

Guided Labs

Cloud is a vast topic and unlike many of Computer Science basic and foundational

classes, it is simply impractical to cover all the material in lecture hours as well as

practically apply them. The other challenge is, as much as we will attempt to discuss

cloud computing topics in this course in a vendor agnostic way, it’s impractical to

get any hands-on experience without actually utilizing a vendor (the irony of cloud!)

Hence, this course will automatically enroll you in AWS Learning Academy through

the A&M’s partnership with AWS. The online courses will give you access to lab work

activities which are designed to help you better understand and apply cloud

concepts and are crucial to developing skills that are key for entry level cloud

practitioners to perform in their first year of any developer’s job. Lab exercises are

to be completed individually and submitted by due date.

Knowledge Checks

Knowledge Checks are multiple choice questions to be completed online every

week after completing the modules. The knowledge checks are available online for

24 hours, you can take them anytime in the day and submit them. It is made up of

10 to 20 questions. These knowledge checks cover the module topics, so you must

watch the videos and read the materials for each module before you attempt the

knowledge checks. Sufficient time is provided so that time is not a problem in

completing the knowledge checks. You are prohibited from using online tools or AI

to complete these knowledge checks.

Course Project

The course project is a multi-phase, open-ended team-based project in which

teams will choose a system to design and implement as a cloud-native software

system. Students will work together to design, architect, and deploy the application

on a public cloud platform. The phases will progressively help the teams define

system requirements, decompose the system into microservices, design service

APIs, and map architectural components to appropriate cloud services.

The project emphasizes cloud architecting principles, and DevOps practices by

establishing a continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipeline

on a public cloud platform. The phases will progressively help the teams define

system requirements, decompose the system into microservices, design service

APIs, and map architectural components to appropriate cloud services.

The project emphasizes cloud architecting principles, and DevOps practices by

establishing a continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) pipeline

that supports independent service deployment.

Here is the grading breakdown of the different project phases:

Course Project Phases

​Project Phase

Weight

Phase 0 – Team Formation and Project Pitch

5%

Phase 1 – System Requirements and Services Decomposition

25%

Phase 2 – CI/CD pipeline and Cloud Services Mapping

20%

Phase 3 – Final System Implementation and Deployment

50%

Total Possible Score

100%

Peer reviews will be part of the project assessment process, 30% of the final project

grade will be allocated to individual contribution.

GitHub setup:

The course project will be developed using a CI/CD pipeline that will start from

GitHub, each project team must create a  private  repository on GitHub. The

professor must be granted access to the repository to monitor the project activities.

The repository is a documentation of the development process, hence teams must

have clear commit messages documenting all changes made along the way. The

GitHub usage will impact the project grade. For example, code added to the

repository and submitted within a short period (the magic of going from no code to

a working solution quickly) will result in extensive deduction of points. The GitHub

will also be used to monitor individual team members contributions.

Attendance and Active Classroom Participation

Coming prepared for class is defined as having studied the material

covered in the previous class, arriving to class on time.

10% of the course grade will be assigned to attendance, active classroom

participation and general classroom behavior and etiquette. Here are the

things that can result in points deducted

Missing more than 2 classes without prior notification and acceptable

absence (See attendance policy section)

Constant cell phone use

Arriving more than 5 minutes late for class

Having earbuds/headphones on (even if not being used)

Browsing Internet, playing games or working on other course work

during class time

Random walks in and out of class

The following grading scale is based on the total average weighted grades earned

over the course of the semester.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Each student gets  one free pass  for a late submission (up to 3 days) with no

penalty. The free pass must be claimed before the deadline (student must

email the professor). One day late submission will have 10% points deducted,

two days late submission will have 20% points deducted. No later

submissions will be graded. The free pass can be reclaimed for extra credit at

the end of the semester if never utilized (great deal!)

Students with 504 accommodations will get the necessary accommodations

per their accommodation letter

AI Statement

Unless indicated in the assignment or project and authorized by the professor, the use of

generative AI to complete the work will be considered a violation of academic integrity. If

in doubt about what it means or counts as a utilization of generative AI, ask the professor

prior to attempting the task to avoid any unexpected penalties, in other words: Ask for

permission instead of forgiveness.
